text: The surgical team begins the hepatic resection using the da Vinci robotic system, with the assistant positioned between the patient's legs. A pneumoperitoneum is established through a five-centimeter incision with an Alexis retractor. Four eight-millimeter robotic trocars and a twelve-millimeter laparoscopic trocar are inserted. The hepatogastric ligament is opened, and a Foley catheter is placed around the hepatic pedicle. An intraoperative ultrasound locates a three-centimeter lesion in the right hepatic lobe, guiding the atypical hepatectomy of segments seven and eight. The coronary ligament is cut, and parenchymal transection is performed using a monopolar hook, bipolar forceps, and vessel sealer. Hemlock clips are applied to major portal and hepatic veins for hemostasis. Two intermittent intracorporeal Pringle maneuvers are used during transection. Subsegmental portal pedicles and hepatic veins are identified, clipped, and sectioned to complete the resection.

After achieving hemostasis, the surgical specimen is extracted through the fan and steel incision. The procedure continues with the rectal resection, repositioning robotic ports one and two with a twelve millimeter and eight millimeter trocar. The robotic boom is adjusted for a new target anatomy. An extended medial-to-lateral approach is performed, respecting the Girauda fascia, which allows identification of the left ureter. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected 1.5 centimeters from the aorta, clipped with Hemolock, and sectioned. The inferior mesenteric vein is similarly dissected, clipped, and sectioned. Dissection proceeds to visualize the lower border of the pancreas, followed by splenic flexure mobilization. The superior surface of the pancreas is separated from the retroperitoneum, and the lesser sac is opened. The left colon is mobilized by dissecting the Toldt fascia using monopolar scissors and a vessel sealer.

The surgeon begins the partial mesorectal excision by dissecting the posterior plane, followed by the right and left planes. The tumor is visible on the anterior surface of the high rectum. The mesorectum is dissected five centimeters distal to the rectal lesion. The procedure concludes with a stapled end-to-end colorectal anastomosis, and a diverting ileostomy is created at the site of robotic port number two.
